title: Gaza — Israel–Hamas War
summary: The conflict's roots run back more than a century. Ottoman, then British-administered Palestine saw growing Jewish immigration and Arab-Jewish tension from the early 1900s, formalized by Britain's 1917 Balfour Declaration backing a 'national home for the Jewish people' alongside a British pledge to protect the rights of the existing Arab population. The United Nations' 1947 plan to partition the territory into Jewish and Arab states was accepted by Jewish leaders and rejected by Arab leaders and neighboring states; the 1948 war that followed Israel's declaration of independence is remembered by Israelis as the War of Independence and by Palestinians as the Nakba ('catastrophe'), in which roughly 700,000 Palestinians fled or were expelled from what became Israel and were never permitted to return.

Egypt administered Gaza and Jordan the West Bank from 1948 until Israel captured both, along with East Jerusalem and the Sinai and Golan, in the 1967 Six-Day War — beginning an occupation of the Palestinian territories that continues in the West Bank today. The 1993–95 Oslo Accords created a Palestinian Authority with limited self-rule in parts of the West Bank and Gaza but left the hardest questions — Jerusalem, refugees, settlements, final borders — unresolved, and a second uprising (the 2000–2005 Intifada) hardened positions on both sides. Israel withdrew its troops and roughly 8,000 settlers from Gaza in 2005; after Hamas won the 2006 Palestinian elections and then violently seized full control of Gaza from the rival Fatah party in 2007, Israel and Egypt imposed a blockade — which Israel says is necessary to stop weapons reaching Hamas and which the UN and rights groups have long called collective punishment of Gaza's civilian population. Four shorter Israel-Hamas wars, in 2008–09, 2012, 2014, and 2021, preceded the far larger war that began in 2023.

The war began on 7 October 2023, when Hamas and allied militants crossed from Gaza into southern Israel, killing around 1,200 people, mostly civilians, and taking roughly 250 people hostage. Israel responded with a large-scale air and ground campaign in Gaza aimed at dismantling Hamas's military and governing capacity and recovering the hostages. The attack followed decades of unresolved conflict over the occupation, the blockade of Gaza, and the status of a future Palestinian state.

The campaign has killed tens of thousands of Palestinians, according to Gaza health officials, and displaced the overwhelming majority of the strip's roughly two million residents, many of them repeatedly. Large areas of Gaza's cities have been reduced to rubble, and Israel's blockade has restricted the entry of food, fuel, and medical supplies, producing famine conditions in parts of the territory. Successive ceasefire and hostage-release negotiations, mediated chiefly by Qatar, Egypt, and the United States, have produced only partial and temporary halts to the fighting.

The war has destabilized the wider region, drawing in Hezbollah in Lebanon, Houthi attacks on shipping from Yemen, and direct Israel-Iran confrontation, while fueling protests and diplomatic rifts worldwide. Rebuilding Gaza, agreeing on who will govern it, and the fate of remaining hostages and detainees remain the central unresolved questions, with a lasting settlement to the broader Israeli-Palestinian conflict still elusive.

title: Ukraine — Russian Invasion
summary: Russia launched a full-scale invasion of Ukraine on 24 February 2022, expanding a war that had already been underway in eastern Ukraine's Donbas region and Crimea since 2014. Moscow's stated aims included preventing Ukraine's alignment with NATO and the European Union and asserting claims over Ukrainian territory and identity; Kyiv and most of the international community rejected these as a war of aggression in violation of international law.

Ukrainian forces halted Russia's initial drive on Kyiv and have since fought a grinding war of attrition along a front line running through the east and south, marked by trench warfare, drone strikes, and long-range missile attacks on cities and infrastructure on both sides. Russia occupies roughly a fifth of Ukrainian territory, including Crimea and parts of four other regions it formally annexed in 2022, though full control of those regions is contested.

The war has caused hundreds of thousands of military casualties on both sides and forced millions of Ukrainians to flee abroad, mostly to Europe, while millions more are displaced within the country. It has also disrupted global grain and energy markets, prompted sweeping Western sanctions on Russia, and made Ukraine's future security guarantees — including any path to NATO or EU membership — a central question in periodic negotiations over how the war might end.

title: Syria — War & Transition
summary: Syria's civil war began in 2011 after security forces violently suppressed peaceful protests that were part of the wider Arab Spring, escalating into an armed uprising against President Bashar al-Assad's government. The conflict drew in a fragmented array of rebel and jihadist factions, Kurdish forces, and foreign powers including Russia, Iran, Turkey, and a US-led coalition against the Islamic State, killing hundreds of thousands of people over more than a decade.

The Assad government fell in December 2024 when a rebel offensive led by the Hayat Tahrir al-Sham group swept from the northwest and captured Damascus within days, ending more than five decades of Assad family rule. A transitional administration has since sought to consolidate control, unify the country's armed factions, and win international recognition and sanctions relief, while facing outbreaks of sectarian violence against Alawite, Druze, and other minority communities.

Syria remains one of the world's largest displacement crises, with millions of refugees abroad, chiefly in Turkey, Lebanon, and Jordan, and millions more displaced inside the country. Rebuilding a shattered economy and infrastructure, integrating former rebel groups into a national army, and managing the return of refugees are likely to shape Syria's transition for years, even as the fall of Assad removed one of the region's longest-standing dictatorships.

title: Yemen — Civil War & Hunger Crisis
summary: Yemen's civil war escalated in 2014–2015 when the Houthi movement, drawing on a base in the north, seized the capital Sanaa and forced the internationally recognized government to flee, prompting a Saudi Arabia-led coalition to intervene militarily on the government's behalf. The war compounded Yemen's existing poverty and fragile institutions, splitting the country between Houthi-controlled areas in the north and government and separatist-held areas in the south.

A UN-brokered truce in 2022 significantly reduced large-scale fighting, and Saudi Arabia and the Houthis have since held on-and-off talks toward a broader settlement, though a comprehensive peace deal has not been finalized. Since late 2023, the Houthis have also launched missile and drone attacks on shipping in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden in solidarity with Palestinians in Gaza, drawing US and allied naval strikes and threatening a key global trade route.

Years of war have left Yemen's health, water, and economic systems in collapse, and aid organizations have repeatedly ranked it among the world's worst humanitarian crises, with millions of people acutely food insecure and dependent on international assistance that has been shrinking due to funding shortfalls. A durable peace remains elusive, with the country still effectively divided and its economy dependent on foreign aid and remittances.

title: Sahel — Insurgency & Coups
summary: Instability in the central Sahel traces to the 2012 Tuareg rebellion and the collapse of Libya after Muammar Gaddafi's fall, which flooded the region with weapons and fighters. Groups affiliated with al-Qaeda and the Islamic State subsequently built strongholds across Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger, exploiting weak state presence, ethnic tensions, and competition over land and resources to expand their reach.

Frustration over the deteriorating security situation fueled a wave of military coups — in Mali in 2020 and 2021, Burkina Faso in 2022, and Niger in 2023 — bringing juntas to power that expelled French and other Western militaries and UN peacekeepers, turning instead to Russian-linked mercenary forces, including the Wagner Group and its successor Africa Corps. The three juntas formed the Alliance of Sahel States and withdrew from the West African regional bloc ECOWAS.

Despite this new military cooperation, jihadist violence has continued to intensify, with insurgents besieging towns, blockading supply routes, and increasingly attacking targets toward coastal states such as Benin, Togo, and Ivory Coast. Millions of people across the three countries have been displaced, and large swathes of territory remain outside effective government control, making the central Sahel one of the deadliest and fastest-growing conflict zones in the world.

title: Ethiopia — Post-Tigray Instability
summary: Ethiopia's current fractures sit atop a much longer history. An ancient empire never colonized by a European power, it came under the Marxist Derg dictatorship after army officers overthrew Emperor Haile Selassie in 1974, then endured a brutal 17-year civil war until a coalition led by the Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) toppled the Derg in 1991. The new government redrew Ethiopia's internal map into ethnically defined regional states — intended to grant self-rule to the country's more than 80 ethnic groups, though critics say it also hardened ethnicity as the basis of political power — and the TPLF dominated national politics for the next 27 years, until mass protests (largely by Oromo and Amhara youth) forced a change in leadership and brought reformist Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed to power in 2018.

War broke out in Ethiopia's northern Tigray region in November 2020 between the federal government and the Tigray People's Liberation Front, which had dominated Ethiopian politics for decades before Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed took office. The two-year conflict, which also drew in Eritrean forces, killed an estimated several hundred thousand people through fighting, famine, and atrocities before a November 2022 peace agreement halted major combat.

Peace in Tigray did not extend to the rest of the country: since 2023, fighting has spread through the Amhara region, where the Fano militia — former government allies during the Tigray war — turned against federal forces amid disputes over regional autonomy and the integration of militia fighters into the national army. Unrest and insurgent violence have also persisted in the Oromia region, driven by longstanding grievances of the Oromo ethnic group.

The compounding conflicts, alongside repeated droughts linked to climate change, have left tens of millions of Ethiopians facing food insecurity and in need of humanitarian assistance. Ethiopia's federal system, built around ethnically defined regions, continues to strain under the competing demands of its many ethnic groups, leaving the country's stability, and that of the wider Horn of Africa, uncertain.

title: DR Congo — Eastern Conflict
summary: Congo's turmoil traces back to King Leopold II of Belgium's personal rule over the Congo Free State (1885–1908), a forced-labor regime built on the rubber trade that killed millions of Congolese and stands among history's most exploitative colonial projects; Belgium then ruled it directly as a colony until independence in 1960. Independence was followed almost immediately by chaos: Belgian officers and Western intelligence services backed the ouster and assassination of the country's first elected prime minister, Patrice Lumumba, clearing the way for Mobutu Sese Seko's three-decade Cold War-era dictatorship. Mobutu's 1997 overthrow triggered the First and Second Congo Wars (1996–2003) — sometimes called 'Africa's World War' for drawing in nine neighboring armies and killing an estimated five million people, mostly from disease and hunger — and left the mineral-rich east chronically unstable ever since.

Conflict in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo has simmered since the 1990s, rooted in the aftermath of the Rwandan genocide, competition over the region's vast mineral wealth — including gold, tin, and coltan used in electronics — and a patchwork of more than a hundred armed groups. The M23 rebel movement, composed largely of ethnic Tutsi fighters and widely reported by UN experts to be backed by neighboring Rwanda, resurfaced in 2021 after a decade of dormancy.

M23 has since seized large parts of North Kivu and South Kivu provinces, including the regional capitals Goma and Bukavu, in offensives that have humiliated the Congolese army and the UN peacekeeping force MONUSCO. Congo has accused Rwanda of direct military involvement to control mining areas, an allegation Rwanda denies, and the two countries have repeatedly traded blame while regional and international mediation efforts, including talks hosted by the United States and Qatar, have struggled to produce a lasting ceasefire.

The fighting has displaced millions of people within Congo and triggered widespread reports of massacres, sexual violence, and looting of mineral resources. Despite the scale of the crisis — among the largest displacement and hunger emergencies in the world — it draws comparatively little international media coverage or funding relative to conflicts of similar magnitude elsewhere.

title: Sudan — Civil War
summary: Sudan's instability has deep colonial-era roots. Anglo-Egyptian rule (1899–1956) governed the mostly Arab, Muslim north and the ethnically African, more Christian and animist south as separate territories before fusing them into one state at independence, leaving unresolved tensions that erupted into Africa's longest civil war between north and south almost immediately — fought in two phases (1955–1972 and 1983–2005) that killed an estimated two million people before ending with South Sudan's 2011 secession. Darfur, in the west, erupted into its own war in 2003 when Sudan's government armed Arab militias — the Janjaweed, the direct forerunner of today's RSF — against non-Arab rebel groups, in a campaign the US government and other observers later determined amounted to genocide.

War broke out in Sudan in April 2023 between the Sudanese Armed Forces, led by General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan, and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), led by Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo, after the two generals — former allies who had jointly ousted a civilian-led transitional government in 2021 — fell out over plans to integrate the RSF into the regular army. The RSF traces its roots to the Janjaweed militias implicated in atrocities in Darfur in the 2000s.

Fighting has devastated the capital Khartoum and spread across much of the country, with the RSF seizing most of Darfur and parts of the west while the army has held out in the east and parts of the center. Both sides have been accused of atrocities against civilians, including ethnically targeted killings in Darfur that the United States and other observers have labeled genocide, and each has received backing from competing foreign states accused of supplying weapons.

The war has displaced more than ten million people, the largest displacement crisis in the world, and pushed parts of the country into famine, according to UN-backed assessments. Despite the scale of the catastrophe, Sudan's war has received only a fraction of the international media attention and humanitarian funding directed at conflicts such as Ukraine or Gaza, prompting aid agencies to repeatedly call it one of the most neglected crises on Earth.

title: Haiti — Gang Violence & Instability
summary: Haiti's current crisis traces to the 2021 assassination of President Jovenel Moïse, which left the country without an elected government and accelerated a collapse of state authority that armed gangs moved to fill. By 2024, gang coalitions controlled most of the capital, Port-au-Prince, effectively besieging the national palace, main port, and international airport and forcing the resignation of Prime Minister Ariel Henry.

A transitional presidential council has since nominally governed the country, backed by a Kenya-led multinational security support mission authorized by the UN Security Council to help Haiti's overwhelmed police force confront the gangs. Progress has been limited: gangs continue to control large parts of the capital and have expanded into previously unaffected regions, while repeated efforts to organize elections have stalled amid the insecurity.

The violence has driven hundreds of thousands of Haitians from their homes, fueled acute hunger, and triggered waves of migration and deportation disputes with neighboring countries and the United States. With state institutions hollowed out and gangs entrenched, restoring basic security and a path to elected government remains a distant prospect for the hemisphere's poorest country.

title: Myanmar — Civil War
summary: Myanmar's conflicts predate the 2021 coup by decades. British colonial rule, formalized in 1886, drew the borders of a state containing more than 100 ethnic groups, and within a year of independence in 1948 several of them — including Karen, Kachin, and Shan forces seeking autonomy or independence — had taken up arms, beginning what is often called the world's longest-running set of civil wars. The military ruled the country directly for most of the period since a 1962 coup, violently suppressed a nationwide pro-democracy uprising in 1988, and only began a gradual, incomplete political opening after 2011, allowing Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy to contest and win elections during the 2010s.

Myanmar's military seized power in a February 2021 coup, overthrowing the elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy shortly after it won a landslide re-election. Mass peaceful protests met a violent crackdown, and many protesters and former lawmakers subsequently took up arms, forming People's Defence Forces aligned with a shadow National Unity Government.

The junta's forces have since fought on multiple fronts against these new resistance forces and against long-established ethnic armed organizations, some of which control substantial territory along Myanmar's borders. A rare alliance of ethnic armies launched a major offensive in 2023 that seized swaths of territory and infrastructure, exposing the military's overstretched position, though the junta has hit back with air strikes on civilian areas.

The war has displaced millions of people, wrecked the economy, and left the junta in control of a shrinking share of the country's territory while still holding most major cities. A devastating 2025 earthquake compounded the humanitarian toll in a country where aid access is already tightly restricted, leaving Myanmar's civil war among the world's least-covered major conflicts despite its scale.

title: Central African Republic — Protracted Conflict
summary: The Central African Republic has cycled through crisis since a 2013 coup by the mostly Muslim Séléka rebel coalition triggered a brutal sectarian backlash from Christian and animist "anti-balaka" militias, killing thousands and displacing a large share of the population. Despite a 2019 peace agreement among armed groups, fighting has continued in various forms ever since.

The government, led by President Faustin-Archange Touadéra, has relied heavily on Russian mercenaries — first the Wagner Group, later reorganized as Africa Corps — to fight rebel coalitions and secure access to the country's diamond and gold mines, arrangements that have drawn accusations of abuses against civilians. A patchwork of armed groups still controls significant rural territory outside the capital, Bangui.

One of the world's poorest and least developed countries even before the crisis, the Central African Republic now has roughly half its population in need of humanitarian aid, with recurring displacement, weak infrastructure, and sporadic violence against civilians that rarely reaches international attention.

title: Cameroon — Anglophone Crisis
summary: Cameroon's Anglophone crisis grew out of long-standing grievances among the English-speaking minority in the country's Northwest and Southwest regions, a legacy of British and French colonial rule that left Cameroon with two official languages and legal systems. Protests by lawyers and teachers in 2016 over the marginalization of English-language institutions were met with a heavy security crackdown, and by 2017 armed separatist groups had emerged seeking an independent state they call "Ambazonia."

The conflict since has settled into a grinding, low-intensity war between government forces and a fragmented array of separatist militias, marked by village raids, kidnappings, and government-imposed lockdowns that separatists have used to enforce school boycotts, crippling education for an entire generation of children in the two regions. Peace talks have made little headway, and both sides have been accused of serious abuses against civilians.

The fighting has killed thousands of people and displaced close to a million within Cameroon and into neighboring Nigeria, yet it remains one of the most neglected crises globally, receiving comparatively little international media coverage, diplomatic attention, or humanitarian funding relative to its toll.

title: The Holocaust
summary: Nazi persecution of Jews began as soon as Adolf Hitler took power in Germany in 1933, with boycotts, the 1935 Nuremberg Laws stripping Jews of citizenship, and the 1938 Kristallnacht pogrom that destroyed Jewish homes, businesses, and synagogues across the country. As Germany invaded and occupied much of Europe from 1939, it forced Jewish populations into overcrowded ghettos and subjected them to forced labor, starvation, and disease.

After Germany invaded the Soviet Union in 1941, mobile killing squads called Einsatzgruppen began mass shootings of Jews and other targeted groups across occupied Eastern Europe. In January 1942, senior Nazi officials met at the Wannsee Conference to coordinate the 'Final Solution' — the systematic murder of Europe's Jews — and built dedicated extermination camps, including Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, and Sobibor, where victims were killed in gas chambers on an industrial scale.

By the war's end in 1945, roughly six million Jews had been murdered — about two-thirds of Europe's pre-war Jewish population — alongside millions of Roma, disabled people, Soviet prisoners of war, political dissidents, and gay men, bringing the total death toll to an estimated eleven million. Soviet, American, and British forces liberated the camps in 1944–45, revealing the scale of the atrocity to the world.

The Holocaust's aftermath reshaped international law and politics: the Nuremberg trials established that following orders was no defense against charges of crimes against humanity, the 1948 Genocide Convention codified genocide as an international crime, and the atrocity became a central argument for the creation of the state of Israel later that year. Holocaust remembrance and education remain central to how many countries teach the dangers of state-sponsored hatred.

title: Rwandan Genocide
summary: Belgian colonial administrators hardened a loose social distinction between Rwanda's Hutu majority and Tutsi minority into fixed ethnic identities, issuing ID cards and favoring Tutsi for decades before independence, then abruptly backing a Hutu-led revolution in 1959 that drove many Tutsi into exile. Periodic ethnic violence followed for decades, and in 1990 Tutsi exiles based in Uganda, organized as the Rwandan Patriotic Front (RPF), invaded northern Rwanda, beginning a civil war that hardened Hutu extremist politics.

On 6 April 1994, a plane carrying Rwanda's Hutu president was shot down, and within hours Hutu Power extremists — who had spent months stockpiling weapons and drawing up target lists — launched a coordinated campaign of mass killing against Tutsi civilians and moderate Hutu, organized through the government, army, and Interahamwe militia and incited over radio broadcasts. Over roughly 100 days, an estimated 800,000 people were killed, overwhelmingly with machetes and clubs at close range in what became one of the fastest mass killings in modern history.

The United Nations peacekeeping force in Rwanda, commanded by General Roméo Dallaire, was denied reinforcement and a stronger mandate as the killing unfolded, and most Western governments withdrew personnel rather than intervene. The genocide ended only when the RPF, advancing from the north, captured the capital Kigali in July 1994; roughly two million Hutu, including many perpetrators, then fled into refugee camps in neighboring Zaire, destabilizing the region and helping trigger the First and Second Congo Wars.

Rwanda has since prosecuted tens of thousands of suspects through the UN-backed International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda and community-based gacaca courts, and the RPF's Paul Kagame, president since 2000, has overseen rapid economic development alongside an increasingly authoritarian government. The genocide's legacy still shapes regional politics, including Rwanda's contested role in ongoing conflict in eastern DR Congo.

links.label: UN Outreach Programme on the Rwanda Genocide

title: Cambodian Genocide — Khmer Rouge
summary: Cambodia was destabilized by the Vietnam War, as US bombing campaigns targeted communist supply lines on Cambodian soil and a 1970 coup plunged the country into civil war. The chaos allowed the communist Khmer Rouge, led by Pol Pot, to grow from a small insurgency into a force that captured the capital, Phnom Penh, in April 1975.

The Khmer Rouge immediately emptied Phnom Penh and other cities, forcing the entire population into rural labor camps as part of a radical plan to build an agrarian utopia from 'Year Zero.' Money, private property, religion, and education were abolished, families were broken up, and the regime systematically targeted intellectuals, professionals, ethnic and religious minorities such as Cham Muslims and ethnic Vietnamese, and anyone associated with the former government.

Over roughly four years, an estimated 1.5 to 2 million people — around a quarter of Cambodia's population — died from execution, starvation, disease, and forced labor, documented at sites such as the Choeung Ek killing fields and the Tuol Sleng (S-21) torture and interrogation center in Phnom Penh.

Vietnam invaded and toppled the regime in January 1979, though the Khmer Rouge continued as an insurgency along the Thai border into the 1990s, propped up in part by Cold War politics that saw the US and China continue to recognize it diplomatically. A UN-backed tribunal, the Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia, began convicting senior Khmer Rouge leaders only in the 2010s, decades after the atrocities.

title: Bosnian War & Srebrenica Genocide
summary: Yugoslavia fractured in the early 1990s as nationalist politics rose after the death of longtime leader Josip Broz Tito. Bosnia and Herzegovina — home to a mix of Bosniak Muslims, Orthodox Christian Serbs, and Catholic Croats — voted for independence in early 1992, prompting Bosnian Serb forces, armed and backed by Serbia and the Yugoslav army, to seize territory and declare their own breakaway republic.

Bosnian Serb forces under political leader Radovan Karadžić and General Ratko Mladić besieged the capital, Sarajevo, for nearly four years — the longest siege of a capital city in modern warfare — while carrying out a campaign of 'ethnic cleansing' against Bosniak and Croat civilians elsewhere in the country, including mass detention camps and widespread systematic rape used as a weapon of war.

In July 1995, Bosnian Serb forces overran the UN-declared 'safe area' of Srebrenica, where a lightly armed Dutch peacekeeping contingent failed to stop them, and murdered more than 8,000 Bosniak men and boys over several days — the worst atrocity in Europe since the Second World War, later ruled a genocide by international courts.

The war ended with the US-brokered Dayton Accords in late 1995, dividing Bosnia into two semi-autonomous entities under a weak central government. The International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia went on to convict Karadžić, Mladić, and other senior figures of genocide and crimes against humanity, but Bosnia remains politically fragile and divided along the ethnic lines the war drew three decades ago.

title: Armenian Genocide
summary: The Ottoman Empire's Armenian Christian minority faced periodic discrimination and massacres in the late 19th century. World War I, and the empire's alliance with Germany against Russia, heightened suspicion among the ruling 'Young Turk' government that Armenians near the Russian front posed a security threat.

Beginning 24 April 1915 — still commemorated annually as Armenian Genocide Remembrance Day — Ottoman authorities arrested and executed hundreds of Armenian political and intellectual leaders in Constantinople, then ordered the mass deportation of Armenians from Anatolia. Deportees were forced on death marches into the Syrian desert with little food or water, and massacres, starvation, and disease killed huge numbers along the way.

An estimated 1 to 1.5 million Armenians died, alongside significant numbers of Assyrian and Greek Christians killed in related campaigns, effectively ending millennia of Armenian presence across most of Anatolia.

The killings helped inspire lawyer Raphael Lemkin to coin the term 'genocide' decades later. More than 30 countries and most historians now formally recognize the events as genocide, but the Turkish government disputes both the term and the death toll, making it one of the most politically contested atrocities of the modern era.

title: Holodomor — Soviet Famine
summary: Beginning in 1929, Joseph Stalin's Soviet government forced the collectivization of agriculture across the USSR, seizing peasant land and livestock into state-run farms. Resistance was especially strong in Ukraine, the empire's most productive grain-growing region, which Soviet authorities viewed with particular suspicion as a center of Ukrainian national identity.

In 1932–33, Soviet officials imposed grain quotas on Ukraine that far exceeded what farms could produce, then sealed the republic's borders to prevent starving peasants from fleeing and sent brigades door to door to confiscate remaining food, even as grain continued to be exported abroad. Many historians describe these actions as a deliberate, man-made famine aimed at breaking Ukrainian peasant resistance and national identity.

An estimated 3.5 to 5 million Ukrainians died of starvation and related causes in what is known as the Holodomor, meaning 'death by hunger' or 'to kill by starvation.' The Soviet government denied the famine was occurring at the time and suppressed accurate mortality figures for decades afterward.

Ukraine and more than 30 other countries now formally recognize the Holodomor as a genocide against the Ukrainian people, a designation Russia rejects. The famine remains a defining event in Ukrainian national memory and a frequent reference point in the modern history of Russian-Ukrainian relations, including the current war.

title: Congo Free State Atrocities
summary: At the 1884–85 Berlin Conference, where European powers carved up Africa among themselves, King Leopold II of Belgium secured personal control of the Congo Free State — not as a colony of Belgium, but as his own private territory, one of the largest personal fiefdoms in history, publicly justified as a humanitarian and anti-slavery mission.

In practice, Leopold's administration ran Congo as a forced-labor extraction operation, first for ivory and then, as global demand exploded, for wild rubber. Private companies and state-sanctioned militias held entire villages hostage — often seizing women and children — to compel men to meet punishing rubber quotas, and mutilated or killed those who fell short; severed hands, taken as proof that ammunition wasn't being wasted, became the regime's notorious symbol of terror.

Combined with introduced disease, famine, and a collapsing birth rate caused by the constant disruption of village life, the Congo's population is estimated to have fallen by roughly half during Leopold's rule — a death toll historians place between 5 and 10 million people, on a scale comparable to the worst atrocities of the 20th century despite remaining far less widely known.

Sustained international exposure, driven by campaigners such as E. D. Morel and diplomat Roger Casement, forced Leopold to relinquish personal control in 1908, after which Belgium annexed the territory directly as the Belgian Congo. The episode is now widely cited by historians as a founding case study in colonial-era genocide and one of the first successful international human rights campaigns.

title: Herero and Namaqua Genocide
summary: Germany colonized present-day Namibia as German South-West Africa in the 1880s, and settlers steadily seized land and cattle from the Herero and Nama peoples, while colonial courts and debt schemes accelerated the dispossession of local herders, fueling deep resentment.

When the Herero rose in revolt in January 1904, followed by the Nama later that year, the German colonial commander, General Lothar von Trotha, issued an extermination order, driving Herero survivors into the waterless Omaheke desert and posting guards at wells and waterholes to ensure those who fled would die of thirst. Survivors of both the Herero and Nama uprisings were subsequently interned in concentration camps, a term and practice historians trace directly to this campaign, where forced labor, starvation, and documented medical experiments killed many more.

An estimated 65,000 to 80,000 Herero — roughly 80% of their population — and around 10,000 Nama, about half of theirs, died, in what historians widely regard as the first genocide of the 20th century.

Germany formally acknowledged the killings as genocide only in 2021, more than a century later, agreeing to fund over a billion euros in development projects in Namibia — though descendant Herero and Nama communities have criticized the deal for excluding them from negotiations and for not constituting direct reparations.

title: Bengal Famine of 1943
summary: In 1943, the Bengal province of British India faced a severe food crisis after Japan's occupation of Burma cut off rice imports, a devastating cyclone destroyed local crops, and wartime inflation and hoarding disrupted grain markets. Historians broadly agree the disaster that followed was less a natural famine than a policy failure.

Britain's colonial government in India, overseen by Winston Churchill's wartime cabinet in London, prioritized shipping and food supplies for the military and for Britain itself, denied repeated requests to divert ships to import grain, and continued exporting rice from India even as Bengal starved, while turning down offers of food aid from the United States, Canada, and elsewhere.

An estimated 2 to 3 million people died from starvation and related disease in Bengal in 1943 — one of the deadliest famines of the 20th century — yet it remains far less globally recognized than contemporaneous atrocities of the Second World War, and Churchill's dismissive wartime remarks about the famine remain a lasting source of controversy.

The catastrophe became a landmark case in economist Amartya Sen's influential later argument that famines are caused less by an absolute shortage of food than by failures of distribution, markets, and political accountability — an analysis he developed in direct response to Bengal in 1943.

title: Circassian Genocide
summary: The Circassians, a Muslim ethnic group native to the northwest Caucasus along the Black Sea coast, resisted Russian imperial expansion for decades during the long Caucasian War, which began in 1817.

As Russian forces closed in on the last Circassian strongholds in the early 1860s, imperial commanders pursued a deliberate campaign of village burning, massacre, and mass expulsion, giving Circassian communities a choice between resettlement deep in the Russian interior or expulsion to the Ottoman Empire. Contemporary accounts describe columns of refugees dying by the tens of thousands from starvation, disease, and exposure while awaiting transport ships on the Black Sea coast.

Estimates of the death toll vary widely, from several hundred thousand to over a million people, with the large majority of the Circassian population — by some estimates as much as 90% — killed or expelled from their homeland by 1864, the year Russia declared the Caucasian War over. Most Circassians today live in Turkey and the wider Middle East rather than the Caucasus, a legacy directly traceable to the expulsions.

Georgia formally recognized the events as genocide in 2011, but Russia does not, and the episode remains largely absent from Western historical memory of 19th-century Europe compared with better-known conflicts of the same era.

title: East Timor — Indonesian Occupation
summary: Portugal abruptly abandoned its centuries-old colony of East Timor in 1974–75 amid political upheaval at home, and the territory briefly declared independence before Indonesia invaded in December 1975 — an invasion carried out with the tacit approval of the United States and Australia, wary of a leftist-led government emerging so soon after the Vietnam War.

Indonesian occupation forces waged a brutal counterinsurgency against the pro-independence Fretilin movement over the next two decades, using forced resettlement, aerial bombardment of civilian areas, and deliberate starvation of the population as tools of war. The 1991 Santa Cruz massacre, in which troops opened fire on mourners at a funeral procession in the capital Dili, was one of the few episodes to draw significant international attention at the time.

Estimates place the death toll from violence, disease, and famine during the occupation between 100,000 and 200,000 people — as much as a third of East Timor's pre-invasion population — making it, proportionally, one of the deadliest conflicts of the late 20th century, despite receiving only sporadic Western media or diplomatic scrutiny for most of its duration.

A UN-supervised independence referendum in 1999 saw nearly 80% of Timorese vote to separate from Indonesia, triggering a final wave of scorched-earth violence by Indonesian-backed militias before international peacekeepers intervened. Timor-Leste formally became independent in 2002, and a UN-backed tribunal later indicted senior Indonesian military officers for crimes against humanity.

title: Guatemalan Genocide
summary: Guatemala's 36-year civil war began in 1960, rooted partly in a 1954 CIA-backed coup that overthrew a democratically elected reformist government and installed a series of military regimes, fueling a leftist guerrilla insurgency drawn heavily from the country's Indigenous Maya majority.

Under General Efraín Ríos Montt in 1982–83, the military launched a scorched-earth counterinsurgency campaign that explicitly targeted Maya communities suspected of guerrilla sympathies, destroying hundreds of villages and carrying out systematic massacres, sexual violence, and forced disappearances. A UN-backed truth commission — and later a Guatemalan court — concluded these acts constituted genocide against specific Maya ethnic groups.

An estimated 200,000 people were killed or forcibly disappeared over the course of the war, the vast majority Indigenous Maya civilians, with US military aid and training to Guatemala's armed forces continuing through much of the worst violence as part of Cold War-era anti-communist policy.

A 1996 peace accord ended the war, and Ríos Montt was convicted of genocide by a Guatemalan court in 2013 — the first time a former head of state was convicted of genocide by a domestic court anywhere in the world — though the verdict was annulled on procedural grounds days later and never fully resolved before his death, leaving the case a lasting symbol of Guatemala's unfinished reckoning with the war.
